feature_name: GPIO pin banking and register structure
description: The GPIO module provides up to 32 GPIO signals organized into two banks of 16 pins each. Control registers are 32-bit and operate on paired banks with bit positions mapping directly to pin numbers. Per-pin control fields follow a consistent naming pattern and bit indexing across all registers. This structure enables predictable access for software and simplifies banked operations. Separate input and output paths are defined for clarity of function.
dependencies: Pin multiplexing configuration; System register map
Remarks: Use Table 1 to locate per-pin control bits and ensure the correct bank is targeted during configuration.

description: Each GPIO pin can be configured as input or output using the DIR01 register. A bit value of 1 selects input and 0 selects output, with all pins defaulting to input after reset. Direction settings are maintained per pin across the two banks. Proper direction must be set before attempting to drive outputs or sample inputs. The register layout allows simultaneous configuration of multiple pins.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; Pin multiplexing; System clock
Remarks: Always program direction prior to using data registers; default inputs may require explicit output configuration for drive.

description: The OUT_DATA01 register directly controls the driven logic state for pins configured as outputs. Writes to OUT_DATA01 set or clear output levels for all affected pins in one operation. For pins not configured as outputs, writes have no effect and reads return the register value, not the external pin level. This enables read-modify-write sequences across multiple bits. Output behavior is strictly gated by the DIR01 configuration.
dependencies: DIR01 configured outputs; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Prefer atomic set/clear registers for concurrent software flows; use OUT_DATA01 when a full-mask update is intended.

description: SET_DATA01 provides an atomic method to drive selected GPIO outputs high without read-modify-write. Writing a 1 to a bit sets only the corresponding output while other bits are unaffected. Reads of the SET bit reflect the associated output drive state. This design supports multi-context software without critical sections on shared lines. It reduces bus traffic and contention for frequent bit assertions.
dependencies: DIR01 configured outputs; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Use SET_DATA01 to assert outputs from ISRs or concurrent tasks to avoid RMW hazards.

description: CLR_DATA01 provides an atomic method to drive selected GPIO outputs low without read-modify-write. Writing a 1 to a bit clears only the corresponding output while other bits are unaffected. Reads of the CLR bit reflect the associated output drive state. This complements the SET mechanism for safe deassert operations under concurrency. It enables deterministic low-latency clearing of lines.
dependencies: DIR01 configured outputs; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Use CLR_DATA01 to deassert outputs from ISRs or concurrent tasks to avoid RMW hazards.

description: IN_DATA01 returns the synchronized state of GPIO pins to the GPIO clock domain. For pins configured as inputs, reads provide the external signal level; for outputs, reads return the currently driven value. This allows software to verify output states and sample inputs consistently. Sampling is banked and bit-indexed identically to other data registers. Synchronization avoids metastability when reading external signals.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; GPIO clock domain
Remarks: When validating external wiring, prefer IN_DATA01 over OUT_DATA01 to observe true pin levels.

description: Each GPIO pin supports configurable interrupt generation on rising edges, falling edges, both, or none. SET_RIS_TRIG01 and CLR_RIS_TRIG01 enable or disable rising-edge detection, while SET_FAL_TRIG01 and CLR_FAL_TRIG01 control falling-edge detection. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock. Software can independently arm or disarm each edge per pin. This granularity enables precise event signaling from external inputs.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; GPIO clock; CPU interrupt controller
Remarks: Program only one edge for simpler servicing, or both edges when required; ensure debouncing is handled in hardware or software.

description: Bank-level interrupt enable bits gate all per-pin interrupt sources within each bank. EN0 controls Bank 0 (pins 0–15) and EN1 controls Bank 1 (pins 16–31). Disabling a bank suppresses interrupts to the CPU while per-pin configurations remain intact. This allows fast masking of large groups of inputs. The same bits also govern EDMA event generation behavior per bank.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller; EDMA event routing; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Use bank enables for coarse-grain masking during critical sections or power transitions.

description: INTSTAT01 reports pending GPIO interrupt events per pin with write-1-to-clear semantics. A set bit indicates a latched event that software must acknowledge. Writing a 1 to a bit clears the corresponding pending status; writing 0 has no effect. This enables precise, idempotent servicing of GPIO events. The register is banked and aligned with the per-pin bit mapping.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller; PSC clock enable
Remarks: When clearing W1C bits, write 1 only to the targeted bits and 0 to all others; avoid read-modify-write sequences on W1C fields to prevent races or unintended clears.

description: The GPIO peripheral can generate synchronization events to the EDMA for DMA-based workflows. GPIO events can therefore trigger data movement or processing chains without CPU polling. Bank enables participate in governing event propagation to the EDMA fabric. This integration supports low-latency reactions to external inputs. Specific event numbers are device-specific and referenced in the data manual.
dependencies: EDMA controller; BINTEN configuration; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Map GPIO-triggered EDMA events to lightweight transfers for timestamping or buffering without CPU overhead.

description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled by default and must be enabled via the local Power and Sleep Controller. The GPIO clock frequency is derived at CPU/6 for this device. All edge detection and input sampling are synchronized to this clock. Software must ensure the clock is enabled before any register access or configuration. Disabling the clock halts interrupt/event generation until re-enabled.
dependencies: Power and Sleep Controller (PSC); System clock
Remarks: Enable the LPSC for GPIO before initialization and consider clock gating during long idle periods.

description: The peripheral supports reduced power modes under control of the PSC. When placed in a low-power state, interrupt generation is suspended while output states are held. Returning to an active state resumes normal detection and signaling. PSC sequencing applies across all peripherals and must be followed. This allows system-level power savings without disturbing output drive levels.
dependencies: PSC; System power policy
Remarks: Gate GPIO during known idle windows but verify wakeup sources are not solely GPIO-dependent.

description: Software resets such as emulator resets do not alter GPIO configuration or signal state. A PSC-invoked reset (clock disable, PSC reset, clock enable) restores default configuration settings. Hardware reset returns configuration and data registers to default states, affecting GPIO signal configuration and outputs. Understanding these reset domains is essential for robust startup. Default direction after hardware reset is input.
dependencies: PSC; Device reset controller
Remarks: After hardware reset, reapply pinmux, enable clocks, and reconfigure direction and interrupts before use.

description: GPIO requires a defined initialization flow after hardware reset. First, perform device-specific pin multiplexing to assign pins to GPIO. Next, enable the GPIO module through the PSC. Then program direction, data defaults, and interrupt control for the required pins. Following this sequence ensures reliable operation and event signaling. Conclude by reading back key registers to verify configuration before enabling interrupts.
dependencies: Pin multiplexing subsystem; PSC; DIR01; Data and interrupt registers
Remarks: Codify the sequence into a reusable init routine and validate with readbacks of key registers.

description: C6452 uses extensive pin multiplexing to maximize peripheral functions in limited packages. Software and hardware configuration at reset determine whether each physical pin is assigned to GPIO. The device data manual provides the mapping details to apply before GPIO configuration. Incorrect pin assignment prevents GPIO from controlling or reading the signal. Proper pinmuxing is a prerequisite for any GPIO feature to operate.
dependencies: Device-specific pinmux registers; Boot configuration; PSC
Remarks: Apply pinmux early and lock configuration before enabling GPIO interrupts or EDMA events.

description: GPIO operation is independent of processor endianness. Register semantics and bit positions are unaffected by byte ordering. This simplifies software portability across big-endian and little-endian builds. No special handling is required in drivers for endianness. The module behaves consistently regardless of CPU data ordering.
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: No endianness-specific adaptations are necessary in the GPIO driver.

description: GPIO peripheral operation is not affected by emulation suspend events such as halts and breakpoints. Signals configured as outputs maintain their state, and detection logic remains defined by the clock enable state. This predictability aids debug sessions without perturbing external interfaces. Care must still be taken with clock gating under emulation. Interrupt connectivity can be verified even during debug.
dependencies: PSC clock state during debug
Remarks: Avoid gating the GPIO clock during critical emulation tests to keep detection paths active.

feature_name: Debug: trigger GPIO interrupts via outputs
description: When a GPIO is configured as an output, software-driven transitions can generate GPIO interrupts. This allows testing of interrupt paths without external stimulus. It is useful for validating ISR hookup and routing. The edge configuration still applies and must be armed appropriately. This method should be disabled in production code to avoid unintended events.
dependencies: Edge trigger configuration; BINTEN; CPU interrupt controller
Remarks: Use controlled test pulses on output-configured pins to validate end-to-end interrupt handling.

feature_name: No interrupt multiplexing with other functions
description: GPIO interrupts are not multiplexed with other interrupt sources on this device. Each GPIO-related interrupt path is dedicated. This simplifies configuration and reduces ambiguity in ISR sources. It also eases verification of routing. Software need only consult GPIO status registers to identify sources.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller
Remarks: Rely on INTSTAT01 and CPU interrupt flags for unambiguous source identification.

description: Separate set and clear registers allow multiple software processes to update outputs without critical-section protection. Each write affects only targeted bits and avoids read-modify-write hazards. This design reduces race conditions when multiple contexts share GPIO control. It supports ISR-safe and task-safe bit manipulation. Using these registers improves real-time determinism.
dependencies: DIR01 configured outputs; System bus
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR over OUT when concurrency is possible to maintain atomicity.

feature_name: Using a GPIO signal as an output
description: GPIO signals operate as outputs when the corresponding DIR bit is cleared to 0. The output state can then be driven using OUT_DATA01, SET_DATA01, or CLR_DATA01 depending on whether a full-mask update or atomic bit change is required. Driving high or low is controlled per pin and is effective only when the pin is configured as a GPIO output. Reads of OUT_DATA01 return the register value, while reads of IN_DATA01 can be used to observe the effective signal state. This separation supports verification and robust control in concurrent contexts.
dependencies: DIR01 configured outputs; PSC clock enable
Remarks: Select SET/CLR for atomic edge-safe updates, and use IN_DATA01 to confirm the external level where wiring permits.

feature_name: Using a GPIO signal as an input
description: GPIO signals operate as inputs when the corresponding DIR bit is set to 1. The synchronized state of the external signal is read via IN_DATA01, ensuring sampling occurs in the GPIO clock domain. For pins configured as outputs, IN_DATA01 returns the value being driven by the device; for open-drain configurations, it reflects the wired-logic result on the line. Per-pin interrupt edge detection can be enabled to generate CPU or EDMA events on transitions. This enables responsive, low-latency handling of asynchronous external signals.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; Edge trigger configuration; GPIO clock domain
Remarks: Use interrupts for responsive detection and poll IN_DATA01 only when latency requirements are relaxed.}
